concepts.title: Independence axioms
concepts.blurb: Whitney's axioms: a non-empty hereditary family $\mathcal{I} \subseteq 2^E$ on a finite set, plus the exchange axiom (I3). Hereditary alone gives a simplicial complex; (I3) is what makes the family a matroid.

concepts.title: Bases, rank, and cryptomorphism
concepts.blurb: Maximal independent sets are bases (all of equal size); the rank function $r : 2^E \to \mathbb{Z}$ is monotone and submodular. Bases, rank, closure, and circuits each give an equivalent axiomatization — one matroid wears many costumes.

concepts.title: Vector, graphic, uniform, transversal matroids
concepts.blurb: The four canonical families. Vector matroids capture linear independence over a field; graphic matroids capture forests of a graph; uniform matroids $U_{r,n}$ are the toy benchmark; transversal matroids encode systems of distinct representatives via Hall's theorem.

concepts.title: Closure, flats, and the geometric lattice
concepts.blurb: The closure operator $\operatorname{cl}(S)$ collects elements that contribute nothing to the rank; closed sets (flats) form a graded, atomistic, semimodular lattice. Birkhoff: geometric lattices and simple matroids are the same data.

concepts.title: Matroid duality
concepts.blurb: Every matroid $M$ has a dual $M^*$ on the same ground set whose bases are the complements of the bases of $M$. The construction is involutive; for planar graphs it recovers the planar-dual graph: $M(G)^* = M(G^*)$.

concepts.title: The greedy algorithm
concepts.blurb: Sort elements by decreasing weight and greedily add each if independence holds. Rado–Edmonds: a hereditary family is a matroid iff this greedy algorithm produces a maximum-weight independent set for every weight function.

concepts.title: The Tutte polynomial
concepts.blurb: $T_M(x,y) = \sum_{S} (x-1)^{r(E)-r(S)}(y-1)^{|S|-r(S)}$ — the deepest invariant of a matroid. Its specializations give the chromatic and flow polynomials, all-terminal reliability, and the random-cluster Potts partition function. Duality: $T_M(x,y) = T_{M^*}(y,x)$.
